plainMeaning: The worshipper bows to the Jina enshrined at one holy town after another — Amizara, Navpallav, Navkhand, Tharad, Taranga and Burhanpur.
recitation: amījhare navapallave, sā(mī) ǁ navakhaṇḍa tharādre ṭhāma | tāraṅge burahānapure, sā(mī)

wordByWord.gloss: at (the shrine of) Amījharā Pārśvanātha (the 'nectar-oozing' Pārśva)
wordByWord.etymology: Locative of place-name amījharā < amī (amṛta, 'nectar') + jharā (jharaṇa, 'oozing, dripping'); an epithet of a miraculous Pārśva image said to exude amṛta.
wordByWord.notes: The locative -e marks 'at/in', the case carried by every place-name in this couplet ('[I bow to the Jina] at …').

wordByWord.etymology: Locative of navapallava < nava ('new/nine') + pallava ('sprout, tender leaf'); a Pārśva shrine titled 'fresh-sprouting / nine-leaved'.
The degree-mark '૦' signals a truncated recurring refrain (cf. the same 'સા૦' in the preceding and following verses); most plausibly sāmī < Sanskrit svāmī ('Lord, master').
wordByWord.notes: Printed verbatim as an abbreviation; the full refrain word is supplied by the reciter from the dhāla's repeating burden, not spelled out here.

wordByWord.etymology: nava ('nine') + khaṇḍa ('piece, section'); name of the famed Navkhanda Pārśva image (associated with Ghogha) reverenced as reassembled from nine fragments.
wordByWord.notes: Here in stem/oblique form heading the second place-cluster.

wordByWord.gloss: at Tharād (town in Banāskāṇṭhā, North Gujarat)
wordByWord.etymology: Locative of the place-name Tharād (older Tharādra-); an ancient Jaina centre of the region.

wordByWord.notes: Generalising the foregoing names: each named town is a 'ṭhāma', a venerable Jina-site.

wordByWord.gloss: at Tāraṅgā (hill-tīrtha of Ajitanātha, Mehsāṇā district)
wordByWord.etymology: Locative of Tāraṅgā, the celebrated hill enshrining Ajitanātha, the 2nd Tīrthaṅkara (temple of Kumārapāla, 12th c.).
wordByWord.notes: The one non-Pārśva shrine of the couplet.

wordByWord.gloss: at Burhānpur (Jaina town on the Tāptī, in the Nimāḍ country)
wordByWord.etymology: Locative of Burahānapura, the Indo-Persian city-name (Burhān + pura, 'town'); a long-standing seat of Jaina temples.

wordByWord.gloss: [Lord] — the same abbreviated refrain, closing the line
wordByWord.etymology: See the first 'સા૦'; the burden recurs to seal each half-line of the dhāla.
wordByWord.notes: Repetition is integral to the chant's rhythm, not a textual error.

literalTranslation: At Amījharā, at Navapallava — Lord …; (the) Navakhaṇḍa (Jina), at Tharād — (every such) place; at Tāraṅgā, at Burhānpur — Lord …
idiomaticTranslation: I bow at Amījharā and at Navpallav, O Lord; to Navkhand and to the holy site of Tharād; at Tāraṅgā and at Burhānpur, O Lord — to the Jina enshrined in each I reverently bow.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This couplet is a single bead-string of six pilgrimage shrines, each name set in the locative ('at …') with the unspoken verb 'I bow' carried over from the dhāla's refrain. Four of the six are Pārśvanātha images known by miracle-epithets — Amījharā ('nectar-oozing'), Navpallav ('fresh-sprouting'), Navkhand ('nine-part') — while Tharād names a venerable North-Gujarat town. The single word ṭhāma ('place/shrine') after Tharād does double duty: it both designates Tharād as a holy 'station' and quietly generalises the whole list — every town named is a ṭhāma worth a bow. The second line widens the compass eastward and southward: Tāraṅgā, the great hill of Ajitanātha (the lone non-Pārśva shrine here, anchoring the couplet to a second Tīrthaṅkara), and Burhānpur on the Tāptī, far beyond Gujarat's heartland. Coming after Sācor, Jālor, Godī-Pārśva, Pāṭaṇ and Ahmedabad in the previous verse, and before Khambhāt, Tārāpur, Mātar and Gandhār in the next, this verse marks the geographic reach of the garland — from the Banāskāṇṭhā frontier (Tharād) out to the Nimāḍ country (Burhānpur). The clustering of Pārśva-epithets is deliberate: by chanting Amījharā, Navpallav and Navkhand together the reciter telescopes many famed wonder-working images of the 23rd Jina into one breath of homage. The verse advances the tīrthamālā not by argument but by accumulation — each name a footstep on a mental circumambulation of the holy map.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse embodies caitya-vandana in its concrete tīrtha mode: veneration of the formless, liberated Jina reached through his localized, named images and their shrines. That four of six names are wonder-epithets of one Tīrthaṅkara (Pārśva) underscores a key Jaina conviction — the object of devotion is one awakened state, however many the icons; the 'nectar-oozing' or 'nine-part' marvels are devotional pointers, not the goal. Naming Ajitanātha (Tāraṅgā) beside Pārśva quietly affirms the unity of all twenty-four Jinas as equal fords. To 'bow at' a place one may never physically reach enacts bhāva-vandana — reverence in intention — and strengthens samyaktva (right faith) through felt connection to the wider saṅgha and its sacred geography.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: A reciter today lets each place-name surface as a remembered or imagined shrine, bowing inwardly at each — converting a list into a moving pilgrimage performed from the prayer-mat. Even unvisited names (Burhānpur, Tharād) become reachable by bhāva, training the mind that devotion is not bounded by travel.

crossReferences.text: Tāraṅgā hill temple of Ajitanātha (built by Kumārapāla, 12th c.)
crossReferences.source: Taranga Jain temple, Mehsana — historical record

crossReferences.text: Aṣṭottarī Tīrthamālā (108 tīrthas) recited in the Pakkhī pratikramaṇa
crossReferences.source: Mahendrasiṁhasūri, in Panch Pratikraman Sūtra – Vidhi Sahit (Anchalgaccha ed.)

sources.title: Taranga (Jain pilgrimage, Ajitnath temple)
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Tāraṅgā as the hill-tīrtha of Ajitanātha and its 12th-century Kumārapāla temple.

sources.title: Tharad, Banaskantha
sources.consultedFor: Location of Tharād as a town/Jaina centre in Banāskāṇṭhā district, North Gujarat.

sources.title: Burhanpur
sources.consultedFor: Identity and Tāptī-valley (Nimāḍ) location of Burhānpur as a historical Jaina temple town.

sources.title: Ghogha (Navkhanda Parshwanath)
sources.consultedFor: Association of the Navkhanda ('nine-part') Pārśvanātha image with the Saurāṣṭra coastal town of Ghogha.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (amṛta; sthāna)
sources.consultedFor: Root-glosses for amī/amṛta ('nectar') in amījharā and ṭhāma < sthāna ('place').

notes: The abbreviation 'સા૦' (with degree-mark) is a truncated recurring dhāla refrain, printed verbatim; it most plausibly expands to 'સામી' (sāmī = svāmī, 'Lord'), but the source book's full refrain word is not confirmed from the verbatim fragment alone — hence the parenthetical (mī) in the recitation and reduced confidence. Place-identifications are secure for Tāraṅgā (Ajitanātha), Tharād and Burhānpur; the Pārśva-epithet shrines Amījharā, Navpallav and Navkhand correspond to well-known miracle-images of Pārśvanātha (Navkhand commonly associated with Ghogha), though the precise image intended by the poet is not fixed by the verse itself. All tokens of the couplet, including both refrain marks, are glossed (100% coverage). Spellings (થરાદ્રે, બુરહાનપુરે, etc.) are kept exactly as in the source orthography.
